title: Writing - Part 1: Digital Privacy
description: Your class has attended a lecture on the growing threats to personal privacy in the digital age and the responsibilities of individuals, companies, and governments.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the concerns in your notes. You should explain which concern you regard as the most serious threat to individual freedom, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Technology companies build detailed profiles of users and sell them to advertisers without proper consent.
Mass surveillance programmes monitor citizens' communications and erode the presumption of innocence.
Weak digital security leaves ordinary people vulnerable to identity theft and financial fraud.

structure.introduction: Introduce the erosion of digital privacy as one of the defining challenges of contemporary life.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the threat posed by corporate data collection and the commodification of personal information.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the implications of government surveillance for individual freedom and democratic society.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both threats and argue which represents the greater danger to personal liberty.

content.modelAnswer:
In an era in which virtually every human interaction leaves a digital trace, the erosion of personal privacy has emerged as one of the most consequential challenges of modern life. Two of the most significant threats—the systematic collection of data by corporations and the surveillance capacities of governments—merit close examination. This essay will assess which poses the greater danger to individual freedom.

The business model of the dominant technology companies rests on the continuous harvesting of personal data. Every search query, purchase, and social interaction is recorded, analysed, and monetised, building intimate profiles of users that are then sold to advertisers or shared with third parties. Most users consent to this arrangement without fully understanding its scope, having accepted lengthy terms of service that are deliberately opaque. Beyond the commercial exploitation of personal information, this data ecosystem creates vulnerabilities: when companies suffer breaches, the intimate details of millions are exposed.

Government surveillance, however, represents a qualitatively different threat. State-operated mass monitoring programmes, documented extensively since the revelations of 2013, collect communications data on an unprecedented scale. Unlike corporate data collection, government surveillance carries the weight of legal authority, and can be deployed to identify, target, and suppress political dissent. The chilling effect on free expression—the self-censorship that occurs when citizens suspect they are being watched—strikes at the foundations of democratic participation.

In conclusion, I regard government surveillance as the more serious threat to individual freedom. Corporate data collection is exploitative; state surveillance, if unchecked, can extinguish the political freedoms upon which all others depend.
